english_expressions.expression: It's on the tip of my tongue.
english_expressions.meaning: 입에서 맴돌아요. / 알 것 같은데 딱 안 나와요.
english_expressions.example: I know the actor's name — it's on the tip of my tongue. Give me a second.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Do you remember the name of that restaurant we went to last year?
B: It's on the tip of my tongue! The one near the river with the outdoor seating... starts with an L, I think.

english_expressions.expression: I'm all over the place today.
english_expressions.meaning: 오늘 정신이 하나도 없어요. / 너무 산만해요.
english_expressions.example: Sorry I keep losing track of what I was saying — I'm all over the place today.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You seem distracted. Everything okay?
B: I'm all over the place today — barely slept, missed my alarm, and I forgot my laptop at home. Today is not my day.

english_expressions.meaning: 그 말이 가슴에 와 닿았어요. / 정말 공감이 됐어요.
english_expressions.example: The part of the documentary about loneliness in big cities really hit home for me.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What did you think of the speech?
B: It really hit home. When she talked about how small moments define who we are — I couldn't stop thinking about my own life after that.

english_expressions.expression: You really went above and beyond.
english_expressions.meaning: 기대 이상으로 정말 애써줬어요. / 크게 노력해줬어요.
english_expressions.example: Staying until midnight to finish the report wasn't expected — you really went above and beyond.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I rewrote the whole proposal and added a competitive analysis on top.
B: You didn't have to do all that — but you really went above and beyond. The client is going to love it.

english_expressions.expression: Let's not beat around the bush.
english_expressions.meaning: 돌려 말하지 말고 솔직하게 얘기합시다.
english_expressions.example: We've been dancing around this issue for weeks — let's not beat around the bush anymore.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Well, there are various factors to consider and the situation is complex...
B: Let's not beat around the bush. Are you saying the project is over budget or not?

english_expressions.meaning: 좀 쉬어야 할 것 같아요. / 잠깐 쉬고 싶어요.
english_expressions.example: I've been staring at this screen for six hours — I could really use a break.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You look exhausted. When did you last step away from your desk?
B: Honestly? I can't remember. I could use a break — even just ten minutes of fresh air would help.

english_expressions.meaning: 말은 쉽죠. / 그게 말처럼 쉬운 게 아니에요.
english_expressions.example: You say just 'be more confident,' but that's easier said than done.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Just stop worrying about what people think of you.
B: That's easier said than done when you're presenting to fifty people who are all experts in the field.

english_expressions.meaning: 계속 소식 전해드릴게요. / 업데이트 해드릴게요.
english_expressions.example: The test results aren't back yet, but I'll keep you posted as soon as I hear anything.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Any news on the apartment application?
B: Not yet — they said it could take a few more days. I'll keep you posted the moment I hear something.

english_expressions.expression: Don't judge a book by its cover.
english_expressions.meaning: 겉모습만 보고 판단하지 마세요.
english_expressions.example: I almost skipped that café because it looked run-down, but don't judge a book by its cover — it was the best coffee I've had all year.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: He seemed a bit awkward in the interview. I'm not sure about him.
B: Don't judge a book by its cover. Once I actually worked with him on a project, his quiet confidence and attention to detail were exactly what we needed.

english_expressions.meaning: 저는 아침형 인간이 아니에요. / 아침에 너무 약해요.
english_expressions.example: Don't expect much from me before 9 AM — I'm not a morning person.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Want to join the 6 AM jogging club?
B: I appreciate the invite, but I'm really not a morning person. I'm barely functional before I've had two cups of coffee. Maybe an evening run instead?

english_expressions.meaning: 길게 얘기하면 복잡하고, 요약하자면...
english_expressions.example: Long story short, we missed the flight, rented a car, and somehow made it to the wedding on time.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How did the job interview go? You were gone for four hours!
B: It's a wild story — long story short, they offered me the position on the spot and I start next Monday.

english_expressions.meaning: 그건 고민할 것도 없어요. / 당연한 선택이죠.
english_expressions.example: If you can get the same quality at half the price, switching suppliers is a no-brainer.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Should we take the direct flight or the one with two stopovers that costs half as much?
B: You've got a meeting at 8 AM the next day — the direct flight is a no-brainer. Pay the extra and arrive fresh.

english_expressions.meaning: 저도 눈치가 있어요. / 제가 그렇게 순진하지 않아요.
english_expressions.example: You're telling me this deal just happened to land in my inbox with no strings attached? I wasn't born yesterday.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: It's a limited-time offer, completely risk-free, and the returns are guaranteed at 30%.
B: I wasn't born yesterday. No investment guarantees 30% returns — what's the catch?

english_expressions.meaning: 좀 봐주세요. / 너무 몰아붙이지 마세요.
english_expressions.example: It's my first week on the job — cut me some slack if I don't know every procedure yet.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You misspelled three things in that email to the client.
B: I know, I'm sorry — cut me some slack. I was typing it up between two meetings and I'll proofread more carefully next time.

english_expressions.meaning: 말보다 행동이 중요합니다.
english_expressions.example: He promises every week that he'll change, but actions speak louder than words.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: He keeps saying he's committed to the project, but the deadlines keep slipping.
B: Exactly. Actions speak louder than words. If he doesn't deliver by Friday, we need to have a serious conversation.

english_expressions.meaning: 제 능력 밖의 일이에요. / 너무 벅차요.
english_expressions.example: I volunteered to lead the international expansion team, but I'm completely in over my head.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How's the new role going?
B: Honestly, I'm a little in over my head. I didn't realize how much legal and compliance work was involved. I might need to bring in some outside help.

english_expressions.meaning: 우리는 아직 표면만 긁은 거예요. / 이게 시작일 뿐이에요.
english_expressions.example: We've identified five pain points, but I think we're just scratching the surface of what customers are really frustrated about.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We've covered a lot of ground today.
B: We've made good progress, but honestly we're just scratching the surface. There's a whole layer of systemic issues we haven't even touched yet.

english_expressions.meaning: 전화위복이에요. / 겉보기엔 나빠 보여도 사실은 좋은 일이에요.
english_expressions.example: Getting laid off felt devastating at the time, but it turned out to be a blessing in disguise — it pushed me to start my own business.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I'm still upset we lost that client.
B: I know it stings, but it might be a blessing in disguise. They were demanding, constantly changing scope, and paying below market rate. Now we have room for a better client.

english_expressions.meaning: 이번엔 다음으로 미룰게요. / 다음 기회에 할게요.
english_expressions.example: I'd love to join dinner tonight, but I'm exhausted — can I take a rain check?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We're all going out for drinks after work. You coming?
B: I'll have to take a rain check — I promised I'd help my sister move this evening. Next time for sure.

english_expressions.expression: You can't have it both ways.
english_expressions.meaning: 둘 다 가질 수는 없어요. / 두 마리 토끼를 잡을 순 없어요.
english_expressions.example: You want maximum speed AND zero risk — you can't have it both ways in product development.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I want to launch next month, but I also need it to be perfect.
B: You can't have it both ways. If we rush to next month, something will break. If we go until it's perfect, we launch in six months. You need to choose.

english_expressions.meaning: 갑자기 번뜩이는 아이디어가 떠올랐어요.
english_expressions.example: I was in the shower this morning and had a lightbulb moment about how to fix the bug.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How did you figure out the solution so fast?
B: I had a lightbulb moment on the train. The whole thing clicked when I stopped trying to force it and just thought about what the user actually needs.

english_expressions.expression: Let's cross that bridge when we come to it.
english_expressions.meaning: 그 문제는 닥쳤을 때 해결합시다. / 미리 걱정할 필요 없어요.
english_expressions.example: What if the investor says no? Let's cross that bridge when we come to it — first let's prepare the best pitch we can.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What happens if we go over budget in Phase 2?
B: Let's cross that bridge when we come to it. Right now we're under budget in Phase 1 — focus on what's in front of us.

english_expressions.meaning: 공감해요. / 충분히 이해해요.
english_expressions.example: You haven't had a day off in three weeks? I feel you — I was in the same situation last quarter.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I'm so tired of being the only one who cares about the quality of the work.
B: I feel you. It's incredibly draining when you're holding a high standard and the people around you aren't matching your energy.

english_expressions.meaning: 제가 어쩔 수 없는 상황이에요. / 제 손을 쓸 수가 없어요.
english_expressions.example: I'd love to offer you a better deal, but my hands are tied — the pricing was set by headquarters.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can you extend the deadline by even one day?
B: I wish I could, but my hands are tied. The client presentation is locked in and the CEO is flying in for it. There's no flexibility on this one.

english_expressions.meaning: 그건 양날의 검이에요. (장점이자 단점이 되는 상황)
english_expressions.example: Going viral on social media is a double-edged sword — massive exposure, but also massive scrutiny.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Making the app free should massively increase our user base.
B: It's a double-edged sword. More users means more growth, but we lose our revenue stream and suddenly need to monetize through ads or convert free users, which is incredibly hard.

english_expressions.expression: Time flies when you're having fun.
english_expressions.meaning: 즐길 땐 시간이 금방 가요.
english_expressions.example: We were supposed to talk for an hour and ended up chatting for three — time flies when you're having fun.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I can't believe it's already midnight!
B: Time flies when you're having fun. This was exactly what I needed — I haven't laughed this hard in months.

english_expressions.expression: Don't put all your eggs in one basket.
english_expressions.meaning: 한 곳에만 모든 것을 걸지 마세요. / 분산이 중요해요.
english_expressions.example: We're 90% dependent on one platform for our revenue — we shouldn't put all our eggs in one basket.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: We're going all-in on TikTok for our marketing.
B: I'd diversify if I were you. Don't put all your eggs in one basket — algorithms change overnight and we could lose our entire audience.

english_expressions.expression: I'll give it my best shot.
english_expressions.meaning: 최선을 다해볼게요. / 한번 해볼게요.
english_expressions.example: I've never done public speaking before, but I'll give it my best shot.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can you present the quarterly results to the board on Thursday?
B: I've never presented to that level before, but I'll give it my best shot. Can you send me some background on who'll be in the room?

english_expressions.meaning: 이건 더 이상 못 참겠어요. / 인내의 한계예요.
english_expressions.example: They canceled on me for the third time this month — that's the last straw.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The supplier missed the deadline again and didn't even apologize.
B: That's the last straw. I've given them three chances. Let's start the vendor replacement process today.

english_expressions.meaning: 저도 이미 그 생각이에요. / 설득할 필요 없어요, 동의하고 있거든요.
english_expressions.example: You're telling me we need more diverse hires? You're preaching to the choir — I've been pushing for this for two years.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I think the team needs more autonomy and less micromanagement.
B: You're preaching to the choir. I've been making that exact argument to leadership for months. The challenge is convincing the people above us.

english_expressions.meaning: 속에 담아두지 말고 털어놓으세요.
english_expressions.example: You've been quiet all day. Whatever is bothering you — just get it off your chest.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: There's something that's been really bothering me about how the team handled this.
B: I'm glad you brought it up. Please, get it off your chest — I want to hear it, and I'm not going to get defensive.

english_expressions.meaning: 그렇게 하지 않는 게 낫겠어요. / 그건 좀 사양할게요.
english_expressions.example: I'd rather not get involved in that conversation — it's political and I have to work with both of them.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Do you want to be the one to tell him his presentation went badly?
B: I'd rather not. Could someone closer to him deliver that feedback? It'd be better received coming from a peer.

english_expressions.meaning: 혼자만의 잘못이 아니에요. / 둘 다 책임이 있어요.
english_expressions.example: Don't just blame him for the argument — it takes two to tango.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The client relationship completely fell apart and it was all their fault.
B: I hear you, but it takes two to tango. Let's look honestly at where our team's communication could have been better before we assign all the blame.

english_expressions.meaning: 다시 생각해보니 망설여지네요. / 마음이 흔들려요.
english_expressions.example: I signed the lease, but now I'm having second thoughts about moving to a new city.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You seemed so certain about the new job offer yesterday. What changed?
B: Honestly, I'm having second thoughts. The salary is great, but the commute is 90 minutes each way and I'm not sure the role is actually what they described.

english_expressions.expression: Every cloud has a silver lining.
english_expressions.meaning: 어떤 힘든 일에도 밝은 면이 있어요. (전화위복 긍정 표현)
english_expressions.example: The pandemic was devastating, but for many small businesses every cloud has a silver lining — they found entirely new customer bases online.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I'm gutted we didn't win the pitch.
B: Every cloud has a silver lining. We learned a lot about how we present our pricing, and the feedback they gave us is going to make our next pitch much stronger.

english_expressions.meaning: 우리가 아슬아슬한 상황이에요. / 위태로운 상태예요.
english_expressions.example: After missing the last two deadlines, our team is on thin ice with the client.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Should we ask for another extension?
B: Absolutely not. We're already on thin ice. One more delay and they'll cancel the contract. We work the weekend if we have to.

english_expressions.meaning: 기대하지 마세요. / 그게 이루어지길 기다리지 마세요.
english_expressions.example: He said he'd respond by noon — but don't hold your breath. He's notoriously slow with emails.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Do you think the CEO will approve our budget request this quarter?
B: Don't hold your breath. They just froze all discretionary spending last week. We might want to plan for a leaner version of the project.

english_expressions.meaning: 제가 너무 일찍 말했네요. / 김칫국 마셨네요.
english_expressions.example: I told everyone the problem was fixed, but I spoke too soon — it came back an hour later.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Did you say the server was stable again?
B: I spoke too soon. It went down again about twenty minutes after I sent that update. I'm sorry — we're still working on the root cause.

english_expressions.meaning: 지난 일은 묻어두고 화해합시다.
english_expressions.example: We've been at odds for months — I think it's time to bury the hatchet and move forward.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Things have been tense between our teams since the product launch dispute.
B: Can we just bury the hatchet? We're on the same side at the end of the day. How about lunch this week, just us?

english_expressions.expression: I'm between a rock and a hard place.
english_expressions.meaning: 진퇴양난이에요. / 어디로도 갈 수 없는 상황이에요.
english_expressions.example: If I say yes to the project, I burn out. If I say no, I risk losing the client. I'm between a rock and a hard place.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Can't you just tell the vendor to push the delivery date?
B: I'm between a rock and a hard place. If we push the vendor, they'll charge us penalties. If we don't, we miss the launch. Neither option is good.

english_expressions.expression: That really struck a chord with me.
english_expressions.meaning: 그 말/그것이 제 마음에 깊이 울렸어요. / 공감이 많이 됐어요.
english_expressions.example: The line about not waiting for the 'right time' to pursue your dreams really struck a chord with me.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Which part of the book resonated with you the most?
B: The chapter on forgiveness struck a chord. I didn't expect it to — but it made me think about some relationships in my own life I've been avoiding.

english_expressions.meaning: 그건 어쩔 수 없이 따라오는 부분이에요. / 원래 그런 거예요.
english_expressions.example: Dealing with difficult clients isn't pleasant, but it comes with the territory in consulting.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I hate how much after-hours communication this role requires.
B: I know. It's frustrating, but honestly it comes with the territory in a client-facing position. The question is whether the other parts of the role make it worth it for you.

english_expressions.expression: You hit it out of the park.
english_expressions.meaning: 완전히 잘 해냈어요! / 홈런을 쳤어요! (크게 성공했을 때)
english_expressions.example: That presentation was flawless — you hit it out of the park.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I was so nervous the whole time. I couldn't tell if they liked it.
B: Are you kidding? You hit it out of the park. Three of the investors came up to me afterward asking for your contact details.

english_expressions.meaning: 잠깐만 참아주세요. / 조금만 기다려 주세요.
english_expressions.example: Bear with me while I pull up the right file — I have about fifty tabs open.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The slides aren't loading...
B: Bear with me — the WiFi in this room is terrible. Let me switch to a hotspot and we'll be good to go in just a moment.

english_expressions.meaning: 제가 너무 흥분해서 (정도를) 넘어버렸어요.
english_expressions.example: Sorry I ranted about the policy for twenty minutes — I got a little carried away.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You kept adding features that weren't in the original brief.
B: I know, I got carried away. Once I started building, I kept thinking 'wouldn't it be cool if...' and just couldn't stop. I'll rein it in going forward.

english_expressions.expression: It's not the end of the world.
english_expressions.meaning: 이게 세상이 끝나는 일은 아니에요. / 그렇게 큰 일 아니에요.
english_expressions.example: You failed one exam. It's not the end of the world — there are retakes and other paths forward.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I can't believe I made that mistake in front of the whole team.
B: It's not the end of the world. Everyone makes mistakes. What matters is how you handle it — own it, fix it, and move on.

english_expressions.meaning: 호랑이도 제 말 하면 온다더니. (방금 얘기하던 사람이 나타날 때)
english_expressions.example: We were just wondering where you'd gone — speak of the devil!
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Has anyone heard from Jake today? He missed the standup.
B: Speak of the devil — here he comes down the hall now.

english_expressions.meaning: 죽으면 그때 자지요. (일·열정을 위해 수면을 희생할 때 쓰는 자조적 표현)
english_expressions.example: Everyone keeps telling me to take weekends off, but I'll sleep when I'm dead — this is a once-in-a-decade opportunity.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You worked all weekend again, didn't you?
B: I'll sleep when I'm dead. We're so close to launching and I genuinely love what we're building. But okay — I promise I'll take a proper break after the release.

english_expressions.expression: There's no such thing as a free lunch.
english_expressions.meaning: 세상에 공짜는 없어요.
english_expressions.example: The influencer deal seemed amazing until we read the fine print — there's no such thing as a free lunch.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: This software is completely free with unlimited storage. Why would they offer that?
B: There's no such thing as a free lunch. They're monetizing your data. Read the privacy policy before you upload anything sensitive.

english_expressions.meaning: 정말 그렇죠. / 완전 동의해요. (강하게 공감할 때)
english_expressions.example: A: This commute is absolutely brutal in rush hour. B: You can say that again — it took me an hour and a half today.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: This project has had more moving parts than anything I've ever worked on.
B: You can say that again. I've been in this industry for fifteen years and I've never seen a scope this complex managed with such a small team.

english_expressions.meaning: 시간을 낭비하고 있어요. / 빨리 시작합시다.
english_expressions.example: Everyone's here and we have a lot to cover — let's get started. We're burning daylight.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Should we wait for the others before we begin?
B: We're already ten minutes late and we're burning daylight. Let's start and catch them up when they arrive.

english_expressions.expression: I take back what I said.
english_expressions.meaning: 제가 한 말 취소할게요. / 아까 한 말 철회합니다.
english_expressions.example: I said the new system was slow, but I take back what I said — it's actually very fast once you know how to use it.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You said you thought the new hire wasn't working out.
B: I take back what I said. I've seen her work this week and she's actually incredibly methodical and sharp. I judged too quickly.

english_expressions.meaning: 신경이 날카로워요. / 불안하고 초조해요.
english_expressions.example: I've been on edge all morning waiting for the board's decision.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You seem really tense today.
B: I'm on edge. The offer for the house is in and we're waiting to hear back. If we lose this one, I genuinely don't know what we'll do — the market is brutal.

english_expressions.expression: I'll take the fall for this.
english_expressions.meaning: 제가 책임질게요. / 제가 비난을 받을게요.
english_expressions.example: If the launch fails, I'll take the fall for it — this was my call to make.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Someone has to explain to the board why we missed the target.
B: I'll take the fall for this. I made the final decision on the strategy and I should be the one to own it. Set up the meeting.

english_expressions.meaning: 우리는 오랜 인연이에요. / 옛날부터 알던 사이예요.
english_expressions.example: Don't worry about being formal — we go way back. Just talk to him like a normal person.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I didn't realize you knew the CEO personally.
B: Oh yeah, we go way back. We were in the same study group in university. You'd never guess it from how formal he is in meetings.

english_expressions.meaning: 진짜 살았어요! / 고마워요, 덕분에 살았어요.
english_expressions.example: You sent me the file right before my presentation — you're a lifesaver.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I thought you might need the extra charger, so I packed one just in case.
B: You're a lifesaver. My laptop was at 4% and the presentation is in ten minutes. Seriously, thank you so much.

english_expressions.meaning: 생각해볼 만한 좋은 포인트네요. / 곱씹어볼 내용이에요.
english_expressions.example: The suggestion to simplify the onboarding instead of adding more features — that's food for thought.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What if the reason users churn isn't the product itself but the fact that we're attracting the wrong users from the start?
B: That's food for thought. It would reframe our entire acquisition strategy. Let me sit with that over the weekend.

english_expressions.expression: I'm not cut out for this.
english_expressions.meaning: 저는 이것에 맞지 않는 것 같아요. / 적성에 안 맞아요.
english_expressions.example: After six months of management, I've realized I'm not cut out for it — I miss doing the actual work.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How are you finding the new leadership role?
B: Honestly? I'm not sure I'm cut out for this. I spend my whole day in meetings and I haven't written a single line of code in three months. I got into tech to build things.

english_expressions.meaning: 오늘은 이만 마무리합시다. / 퇴근합시다.
english_expressions.example: We've hit every item on the agenda — let's call it a day and get some rest.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Should we try to fit in one more review before we wrap?
B: It's already 7:30 and everyone's running on fumes. Let's call it a day. We'll come back fresh in the morning and it'll be better work anyway.

english_expressions.expression: I can't wrap my head around it.
english_expressions.meaning: 도저히 이해가 안 돼요. / 머릿속에서 정리가 안 돼요.
english_expressions.example: The new tax code has so many exceptions — I can't wrap my head around it.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Did you understand the architect's explanation of the new infrastructure?
B: Honestly, I couldn't wrap my head around most of it. Could you send me a summary? I'm a visual person — maybe a diagram would help.

english_expressions.meaning: 반론을 제기하는 거예요. / 일부러 반대 입장에서 말해보는 거예요.
english_expressions.example: I'm just playing devil's advocate here, but what if the data is pointing us in the wrong direction?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: So we all agree the new feature is a priority?
B: Before we finalize — I'm just playing devil's advocate — but who specifically asked for this? Is it a vocal minority or does the data actually back up the demand?

english_expressions.meaning: 일이 본격적으로 잘 굴러가기 시작했어요.
english_expressions.example: Once we got the first investor on board, the ball really got rolling and three more followed within a month.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The early days of the startup sound chaotic.
B: They were. But once we landed that first enterprise client, the ball really got rolling. Word spread fast and suddenly everyone wanted a demo.

english_expressions.meaning: 아무것도 생각이 안 나요. / 머리가 하얘졌어요.
english_expressions.example: I studied everything last night, but in the exam I was drawing a complete blank.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: What was the name of that framework we used in the last project?
B: I'm completely drawing a blank right now. Let me search through my emails — I know I emailed about it at the time.

english_expressions.meaning: 다 지난 일이에요. / 이미 흘러간 일이에요.
english_expressions.example: We had a falling out two years ago, but it's water under the bridge — we've moved on.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Are you sure you're okay working with him after what happened?
B: It's water under the bridge. That conflict was two years ago, we both said things we regret, and we've talked it through. I'm genuinely fine.

english_expressions.expression: I'll turn over a new leaf.
english_expressions.meaning: 새 출발을 할 거예요. / 마음을 고쳐먹을 거예요.
english_expressions.example: This year I'm really going to turn over a new leaf with my health — no more skipping the gym.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: You've said you'll work on your time management before.
B: I know, I know. But I've got an accountability partner now and I've blocked off deep work hours on my calendar. I'm genuinely turning over a new leaf.

english_expressions.meaning: 각자의 취향이 있는 거죠. / 사람마다 다른 거예요.
english_expressions.example: I don't get the appeal of true crime podcasts, but to each their own.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: How can you like pineapple on pizza?
B: To each their own! I love the sweet and salty combination. I'm not going to judge what you put on yours if you don't judge mine.

english_expressions.meaning: 더 이상 어떻게 해야 할지 모르겠어요. / 방법이 다 떨어졌어요.
english_expressions.example: I've tried everything to fix the bug and I'm at my wit's end — do you have any ideas?
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: The client keeps rejecting every version of the design with no specific feedback.
B: I'm at my wit's end with them, honestly. We've gone through eleven revisions. I think we need to schedule a workshop to uncover what they actually want before we design anything else.

english_expressions.meaning: 너무 배고파요. / 엄청 많이 먹을 수 있을 것 같아요.
english_expressions.example: I skipped lunch and it's now 6 PM — I could eat a horse right now.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Should we grab something small before the dinner reservation at 8?
B: Honestly, I could eat a horse. I've been in back-to-back meetings since 8 AM without a proper meal. Let's grab something now.

english_expressions.expression: Don't bite off more than you can chew.
english_expressions.meaning: 감당할 수 있는 것 이상을 맡으려 하지 마세요.
english_expressions.example: You're already stretched thin — don't bite off more than you can chew by taking on this extra project.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: I said yes to the conference talk, the book chapter, and the podcast all in the same month.
B: Don't bite off more than you can chew. You already have a full load. Which one actually matters most for where you want to be in two years? Do that one well.

english_expressions.meaning: 사람이 많을수록 더 좋죠. / 많이 올수록 환영이에요.
english_expressions.example: Bring your coworkers to the team dinner — the more the merrier.
english_expressions.dialogue:
A: Is it okay if my partner and a couple of friends join us for the barbecue?
B: Absolutely — the more the merrier! Just let me know numbers so I can make sure we have enough food for everyone.
